scenarios.title: Package vs. dependency before `npm install`
scenarios.fixed_criterion: package=object, dependency=relationship. Check the manifest and lockfile diff.
scenarios.encounter: An install was requested, but no package has been installed yet

pre_action.target: The current project's package manifest and lockfile
pre_action.side_effect: No external state changes during review
pre_action.risk: Installation may add direct and transitive code plus install scripts
pre_action.rollback: Checkpoint the manifest and lockfile state before installation

scenarios.next_action: Check the dependency name and version range that would be added to the manifest.
scenarios.diagnostic_question: Can the same package be a dependency of project A but not project B?

policy_labels.text: A package is a distributable or installable object; a dependency is a relationship in which that object is required.
Before installation, check the maintainer, license, version, lockfile diff, and script side effects.

scenarios.title: Start a dev server / process, server, and local
scenarios.fixed_criterion: running process, server role, localhost is not cloud. Check the bind address, public directory, and Ctrl-C.
scenarios.encounter: Preparing to start a dev server for a local preview

pre_action.target: A dedicated, secret-free public-demo directory and a designated port on 127.0.0.1
pre_action.side_effect: A local process listens on the port
pre_action.risk: An incorrect bind address or directory may expose files externally

scenarios.next_action: Check the bind address, public directory, and port, then start the local process.
scenarios.evidence: A response from the 127.0.0.1 URL and a closed port after shutdown
scenarios.diagnostic_question: Is localhost:3000 automatically exposed to the internet?

policy_labels.text: A server is a role that accepts connections or requests and returns responses; localhost resolves to the loopback interface.
Do not serve the home or project root; first confirm the bind address, directory, and shutdown method.

scenarios.title: Missing `.env` / environment variables, config, and secrets
scenarios.fixed_criterion: An environment variable is a delivery channel, not all config or automatically a secret. Never print real values.
scenarios.encounter: The app reports that it cannot find a required environment variable

pre_action.target: The required variable name and the current process's config source
pre_action.side_effect: Check only whether the variable exists; do not display its value
pre_action.risk: A secret value may be exposed in a terminal, log, or chat
pre_action.rollback: Review makes no changes; if config changes, preserve the original value before restarting the process

scenarios.next_action: Check only the required variable name and whether it is present, not its value.
scenarios.evidence: Missing or present status without the value, plus config precedence
scenarios.diagnostic_question: Does placing a value in an environment variable automatically make it a secret?

policy_labels.text: An environment variable is name=value data in a process environment; config is the complete set of settings.
Do not print real secrets; use placeholders, presence checks, and revoke or rotate procedures.

scenarios.title: Read-only API call / request and response
scenarios.fixed_criterion: API=contract, endpoint=interaction point. Check the method, target, and status.
scenarios.encounter: Tracing an HTTP API flow that reads one fictional task

pre_action.side_effect: A read that does not alter stored state
pre_action.risk: The wrong method, target, credential, or account may trigger a different action or cost
pre_action.rollback: No changes because this is a read-only fixture

scenarios.next_action: Read the method and target, then trace the expected status and response body.
scenarios.evidence: Request method and target, plus response status and content
scenarios.diagnostic_question: Can an MCP server call an existing GitHub API internally?

policy_labels.text: An API is an interaction contract, an endpoint is a specific interaction point, and HTTP requests and responses are one implementation of that contract.
Before a real call, display the method, target, account, credential type, and potential cost.

scenarios.title: API key error / API key, access token, and LLM token
scenarios.fixed_criterion: Separate the two credential types from the model-processing unit. Rotate exposed secrets.
scenarios.encounter: A placeholder credential was rejected, and three uses of the word token must be distinguished

pre_action.target: Credential type, scope, resource, and model-usage unit
pre_action.side_effect: Review only the contracts, without real secrets
pre_action.risk: Copying a real key or token into a chat, repository, or log may expose the account and incur costs
pre_action.rollback: If exposure is suspected, revoke or rotate the credential with its provider

scenarios.next_action: Without displaying a real value, determine whether the error refers to an API key, OAuth access token, or LLM token.
scenarios.evidence: Credential type, scope or resource, and model token usage shown as separate items
scenarios.diagnostic_question: Can copying a context token log you in to an API?

policy_labels.text: API keys and OAuth access tokens are credentials; an LLM token is a model-processing unit.
Do not repeat a real secret; if it was exposed, recommend revoking or rotating it before merely deleting the copy.

scenarios.title: Database connection error / server, database, and query
scenarios.fixed_criterion: Distinguish a server role from a data system; verify SELECT and the target before any write.
scenarios.encounter: A database connection failed, so the request-handling role and data system must be separated

pre_action.target: The database connection target and a read-only query fixture
pre_action.side_effect: Read the connection settings and review only a constrained SELECT
pre_action.risk: A query can also modify or delete data, and a production write may be difficult to recover
pre_action.rollback: Review makes no changes; separately verify the backup, transaction, and rollback path before a write

scenarios.next_action: Check the database type and connection target, then identify a query that makes no data changes.
scenarios.evidence: The server role, database system, and query behavior shown separately
scenarios.diagnostic_question: Does an application that reads an SQLite file always need a separate database server?

policy_labels.text: A server is a request-handling role, a database is a data-management system, and a query does not necessarily mean read-only.
Do not automatically run a production write query without a backup, transaction, target row count, and rollback path.

scenarios.title: Connect an MCP server / API, MCP, model, and tool
scenarios.fixed_criterion: Protocol fact: model/app selects use → client sends `tools/call` → server executes → result is processed; confirmation is implementation-specific. Product policy: Fairytail separately confirms sensitive tools. Review server trust and permissions.
scenarios.encounter: Reviewing an MCP server's read-only resources and tool list before connecting

pre_action.target: The MCP server under review and its declared resource and tool capabilities
pre_action.side_effect: Read only the list and schemas; do not call a tool
pre_action.risk: A local MCP server can be as risky as its OS, file, or shell permissions and may call external APIs
pre_action.rollback: Do not enable the connection, or disable the server in configuration

scenarios.next_action: First check the server's source, transport, permissions, and read-only resources.
scenarios.evidence: Host, client, server, actual executor, tool and resource lists, and permission scope
scenarios.diagnostic_question: When tool-call JSON is generated, has the file already changed? Which component actually executes the action?

policy_labels.text: A model or application selects use, the client sends tools/call, and the MCP server executes it; confirmation depends on the host implementation and policy.
Fairytail requires a separate allowlist, target display, user confirmation, and schema validation for sensitive tools.

scenarios.title: `permission denied` / AuthN·AuthZ·permission
scenarios.fixed_criterion: Separate identity verification from action authorization. Never use `chmod 777` as a blanket fix.
scenarios.encounter: A specific action on a resource was denied even though the user is signed in

pre_action.target: The denied principal, resource, action, and current permission
pre_action.risk: Broad privilege escalation may grant unnecessary access to other users or processes
pre_action.rollback: Review makes no changes; if permissions change, record the original owner, mode, and duration

scenarios.next_action: Separate the principal, resource, and action, then read the current permission without changing it.
scenarios.evidence: Authentication result, authorization allow or deny decision, and specific permission shown separately
scenarios.diagnostic_question: Can deletion still be denied after a successful login?

policy_labels.text: Authentication verifies identity; authorization decides whether a specific action on a resource is allowed.
Do not immediately propose chmod 777; check least privilege and recovery instead.

scenarios.title: Check the repository and path before editing a file
scenarios.fixed_criterion: Distinguish folder, project, and repository. Check the absolute target and Git status.
scenarios.encounter: Checking the work location and history boundary before an AI edits a file

pre_action.target: The resolved absolute file path and repository root
pre_action.side_effect: Read only the working directory, repository root, and Git status
pre_action.risk: A recursive edit, move, or deletion may run in the wrong directory
pre_action.rollback: Review makes no changes; use the pre-edit Git status as a checkpoint

scenarios.next_action: Check the current path, repository root, and pre-edit Git status.
scenarios.evidence: Absolute target, repository root, and tracked or untracked changes
scenarios.diagnostic_question: Is every project folder a Git repository?

policy_labels.text: A folder is a collection of files, a repository is a Git history structure, and a project is a tool-defined work scope.
Before a recursive edit, deletion, or move, recheck the resolved absolute target and repository root.

scenarios.title: preview deploy / local·remote·cloud·push/deploy
scenarios.fixed_criterion: push is not deploy. Check the target, public exposure, billing, secrets, health, and rollback.
scenarios.encounter: Preparing local work for a secret-free, non-production preview

pre_action.target: The specified non-production preview environment and remote repository
pre_action.side_effect: Review only the plan and target, without public exposure, billing, or deployment
pre_action.risk: Production, public exposure, billing, secrets, or a database migration may be involved
pre_action.rollback: Confirm the existing state and rollback path before a preview release

scenarios.next_action: Read the remote and deployment targets separately, then check public exposure, billing, secrets, health, and rollback.
scenarios.evidence: Push state shown separately from deployment, release, and health state
scenarios.diagnostic_question: Does the service change merely because code was pushed to GitHub?

policy_labels.text: A push transfers Git objects; a deployment is a lifecycle action that applies a build, config, or release to a target environment.
Do not automatically trigger production, public exposure, billing, domain changes, secret handling, or database migrations without explicit approval.
